stage.name: DeFi Mechanics (High School)
stage.description: Liquidity pools, the x·y=k formula, impermanent loss, yield farming, smart contract risks, and DeFi due diligence — intermediate level.

elements.content: No order books. No market makers. Just math — High School Level

Traditional exchanges match buyers with sellers via order books. DEXs use liquidity pools — smart contracts holding two tokens that anyone can trade against. The price is set automatically by a formula, not by humans.

x × y = k

x = token A reserve
y = token B reserve
k = constant product

When you buy token A: its supply in the pool drops → k must stay constant → token A's price rises automatically.
When you sell token A: its supply increases → price falls automatically.
No human sets the price — it's pure math responding to supply changes.

1. Deposit equal value of both tokens (e.g. $500 ETH + $500 USDC)
2. Receive LP tokens = your share of the pool
3. Earn % of every swap fee (usually 0.3%) proportional to your share
4. Withdraw anytime by burning your LP tokens
→ Risk: impermanent loss (next slide)

DeFi Building Blocks

🔄 DEX — Trade tokens from your wallet, no sign-up
🏦 Lending — Deposit collateral, borrow against it
🌾 Yield Farming — Provide LP tokens, earn protocol rewards
⚖️ Stablecoins — $1-pegged tokens to hold value in DeFi

actions.text: Liquidity Pools and the AMM Formula — High School Level. DeFi is banking without banks. Financial services built on smart contracts — no company runs them.

Traditional exchanges match buyers with sellers via order books. DEXs use liquidity pools — smart contracts holding two tokens that anyone can trade against. The price is set automatically by a formula, not by humans.

The formula is x times y equals k. X is the token A reserve. Y is the token B reserve. K is a constant that never changes. When you buy token A, its supply in the pool drops — so k must stay constant, meaning token A's price rises automatically. When you sell, price falls. No human sets the price.

To provide liquidity: deposit equal value of both tokens, for example five hundred dollars in ETH and five hundred in USDC. You receive LP tokens representing your share. You earn a percentage of every swap fee — usually 0.3% — proportional to your share. Withdraw anytime by burning your LP tokens. The risk is impermanent loss, which we'll cover next.

The four DeFi building blocks: DEXs let you trade tokens from your wallet with no sign-up. Lending protocols let you deposit collateral and borrow against it. Yield farming rewards you for providing LP tokens. And stablecoins are dollar-pegged tokens that let you hold value in DeFi without converting back to fiat.

elements.content: The hidden risk of LP positions and what can go wrong in DeFi

When token prices move, the pool rebalances — your share shifts toward the underperforming token. If you had just held instead of providing liquidity, you'd have more of the outperformer. That difference is impermanent loss.
IL is 'impermanent' because if prices return to entry levels, it disappears. It only becomes a realized loss when you withdraw while prices have moved. Swap fee income can offset it.

⚠️ Smart contract bugs — $3B+ lost to DeFi hacks in 2022
⚠️ Rug pulls — Devs drain liquidity pool and disappear
⚠️ Oracle manipulation — Flash loans manipulate price feeds
⚠️ Composability risk — One exploit cascades through stacked protocols

✅ Due diligence: Multiple audits from reputable firms, 6+ months live without incident, growing TVL, open-source code, public team, bug bounty program

XRPL has a native DEX and AMM built into the protocol — no smart contracts needed. Trading has been live on-chain since 2012. OnlyCrypto payouts go directly to your wallet in seconds — no custody risk.

actions.text: Impermanent Loss and DeFi Risks

Impermanent loss: When token prices move, the pool rebalances — your share shifts toward the underperforming token. If you had just held instead of providing liquidity, you'd have more of the outperformer. That difference is impermanent loss.

If the token price doubles — a one hundred percent increase — you experience about 5.7% impermanent loss compared to just holding. If it goes up four times, that's about 20% IL. And at ten times, you're looking at about 42% impermanent loss versus holding.

IL is impermanent because if prices return to entry levels, it disappears. It only becomes a realized loss when you withdraw while prices have moved. Swap fee income from the pool can offset it — the math only hurts you if the price move is large and you exit early.

DeFi risk checklist: Smart contract bugs — over three billion dollars lost to DeFi hacks in 2022 alone. Rug pulls — developers drain the liquidity pool and disappear. Oracle manipulation — flash loans manipulate price feeds to exploit protocols. And composability risk — one exploit in a protocol you've never heard of can cascade through every stacked protocol you're in.

XRPL has a native DEX and AMM built into the protocol — no external smart contracts needed. Trading has been live on-chain since 2012. OnlyCrypto payouts go directly to your wallet in seconds — no custody risk from a third-party smart contract.

elements.content: How to maximize returns across DeFi protocols

Yield farming is the practice of moving your crypto across different DeFi protocols to chase the highest return. A farmer might deposit liquidity in Uniswap, earn LP tokens, stake those LP tokens elsewhere for bonus yield, then reinvest the rewards.


Risks: smart contract vulnerabilities, protocol collapse, and the infamous impermanent loss from liquidity provision. High APY usually means high risk.

actions.text: 🌾 Yield Farming
How to maximize returns across DeFi protocols

Yield farming is the practice of moving your crypto across different DeFi protocols to chase the highest return. A farmer might deposit liquidity in Uniswap, earn LP tokens, stake those LP tokens elsewhere for bonus yield, then reinvest the rewards. Risks: smart contract vulnerabilities, protocol collapse, and the infamous impermanent loss from liquidity provision. High APY usually means high risk.
🌾 Farm. Move capital to best yield
🔁 Compound. Reinvest rewards automatically
⚠️ Smart Contract Risk. Bugs = lost funds
💡 Rule. High APY = high risk, always

elements.content:
Impermanent loss occurs when the price ratio of tokens in a liquidity pool changes from when you deposited. The AMM rebalances your position automatically, meaning you end up with more of the token that fell and less of the one that rose.


Example: you deposit ETH + USDC 50/50. ETH price doubles. You now have less ETH than if you had just held. The loss is "impermanent" because it recovers if prices return to entry ratio — but if you withdraw during divergence, the loss becomes permanent.

actions.text: ⚖️ Impermanent Loss — Deep Dive
The hidden cost of providing liquidity

Impermanent loss occurs when the price ratio of tokens in a liquidity pool changes from when you deposited. The AMM rebalances your position automatically, meaning you end up with more of the token that fell and less of the one that rose. Example: you deposit ETH + USDC 50/50. ETH price doubles. You now have less ETH than if you had just held. The loss is "impermanent" because it recovers if prices return to entry ratio — but if you withdraw during divergence, the loss becomes permanent.
📊 Price Divergence. Bigger gap = bigger IL
💸 IL Formula. Increases non-linearly with price
✅ Mitigation. Use stablecoin pairs (less IL)
⏳ Impermanent. Recovers if prices return

elements.content: What can actually go wrong in DeFi — and what to watch for

DeFi protocols carry risks that traditional finance does not. Before depositing into any protocol, evaluate these key risk vectors:


Smart contract bugs — even audited code can have exploits. Over $3B was lost to DeFi hacks in 2022. Oracle manipulation — price feeds can be manipulated to drain lending protocols. Admin key risk — if a team holds upgrade keys they can change or drain contracts.

🔑 Admin Risk
Team can rug via upgrade keys

actions.text: 🔒 Protocol Risk
What can actually go wrong in DeFi — and what to watch for

DeFi protocols carry risks that traditional finance does not. Before depositing into any protocol, evaluate these key risk vectors: Smart contract bugs — even audited code can have exploits. Over $3B was lost to DeFi hacks in 2022. Oracle manipulation — price feeds can be manipulated to drain lending protocols. Admin key risk — if a team holds upgrade keys they can change or drain contracts.
🐛 Contract Bug. Code exploit drains funds
🔮 Oracle Hack. Price feed manipulation
🔑 Admin Risk. Team can rug via upgrade keys
✅ Check. Audit reports + time-locked admin keys

questions.question: In the AMM formula x × y = k, what happens to token A's price when you buy it from the pool?

options.label: The price decreases because you're adding demand
The price increases because token A's supply in the pool decreases, k must stay constant
The price stays the same
The price is set by the liquidity provider

questions.analysis: When you buy token A from the pool, its reserve (x) decreases. Since k must remain constant, y must increase — meaning you get fewer of token A per unit of B. This is how the AMM automatically raises the price as you buy: less supply in the pool = higher price.

questions.question: What is impermanent loss?

options.label: A fee charged by the DEX for every trade
The loss you make when the whole market crashes
The difference between holding tokens vs providing them as liquidity when prices move
Gas fees that can't be recovered

questions.analysis: Impermanent loss occurs because the AMM rebalances toward the underperforming token when prices move. If you had just held your tokens instead of depositing them in the pool, you'd have more of the one that appreciated. IL is 'impermanent' — if prices return to entry levels, it vanishes.

questions.question: What does a rug pull mean in DeFi?

options.label: A smart contract upgrade that changes fees
Developers abandoning a project and draining the liquidity pool, leaving investors with worthless tokens
An impermanent loss event
A failed governance vote

questions.analysis: A rug pull is when project developers — often anonymous — use hidden contract functions to drain the liquidity pool or mint unlimited tokens and sell them, then disappear. Investors are left with tokens worth essentially nothing. It's the most common crypto scam.

questions.question: Which of these is a key sign of a safer DeFi protocol?

options.label: Very high APY with no explanation of the source
Anonymous team with no audit
Multiple audits from reputable firms, open-source code, and 6+ months live without incident
Protocol launched less than a week ago

questions.analysis: Legitimate protocols have multiple audits from well-known firms (like Trail of Bits or OpenZeppelin), make their code open-source for anyone to verify, and have track records of running without incidents. Very high APY with no explanation usually means unsustainable token emission — a red flag.

questions.question: What is oracle manipulation in DeFi?

options.label: Using a VPN to hide trading activity
Using flash loans to temporarily manipulate on-chain price feeds to exploit lending protocols
Predicting future market prices
Setting prices on a centralized exchange

questions.analysis: Oracles are the price feeds DeFi protocols rely on. Attackers can take out enormous flash loans (borrowed and repaid in one transaction), use them to move prices on a DEX, trick an oracle-dependent lending protocol into thinking collateral is worth more or less than it is, and profit from the exploit — all in seconds.

questions.question: In DeFi lending, what happens when your health factor drops below 1.0?

options.label: You earn bonus rewards
Your position is automatically liquidated
Your interest rate is reduced
The protocol pauses your account

questions.analysis: A health factor below 1.0 means your collateral value no longer covers your debt at the required ratio. Liquidation bots monitor positions 24/7 and will close your position, taking a discount on the collateral.

questions.question: What is an oracle in DeFi?

options.label: A smart contract that stores user passwords
A bridge between two blockchains
A service that feeds real-world price data to smart contracts
A governance token voting system

questions.analysis: Smart contracts cannot access external data on their own. Oracles (like Chainlink) feed price data, weather events, or other real-world information into contracts. Manipulating an oracle can drain lending protocols.

actions.text: Five questions on DeFi mechanics — liquidity pools, impermanent loss, rug pulls, and smart contract risks.
Understanding these risks is just as important as understanding the opportunities. Take your time.
